author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
Treatise on the Soul and Its Origin
originalBook: Treatise on the Soul and Its Origin
title: His Fourth Quotation.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: We have read all about the mother of the Maccabean youths, who was really more fruitful in virtues when her children suffered than of children when they were born; how she exhorted them to constancy, speaking in this wise: “I cannot tell, my sons, how ye came into my womb.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: For it was not I who gave you spirit and soul, nor was it I that formed the members of every one of you; but it was God, who also made the world, and all things that are therein; who, moreover, formed the generation of men; and searches the action of all; and who will Himself of His great mercy restore to you your spirit and soul.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: All this we know; but how it supports this man’s assertion we do not see.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: For what Christian would deny that God gives to men soul and spirit?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: But similarly, I suppose that he cannot deny that God gives to men their tongue, and ear, and hand, and foot, and all their bodily sensations, and the form and nature of all their limbs.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: For how is he going to deny all these to be the gifts of God, unless he forgets that he is a Christian?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: As, however, it is evident that these were made by Him, and bestowed on man by propagation; so also the question must arise, by what means man’s spirit and soul are formed by Him; by what efficiency given to man—from the parents, or from nothing, or (as this man asserts, in a sense which we must by all means guard against) from some existing nature of the divine breath, not created out of nothing, but out of His own self?
